current_conditions.site_name: SOQUE RIVER AT GA 197, NEAR CLARKESVILLE, GA

light_pressure_read.read: High pressure and low cloud cover make this fishable but technical.

stocking_read.read: Recent stocking supports eggs, worms, and attractor nymphs.

angler_analysis.expected_trout_behavior: Expect trout to favor the most comfortable feeding lanes while staying close to cover when light is high.
angler_analysis.presentation_style: Prioritize clean drifts, smaller natural patterns, and stocked-trout attractors where appropriate.

fly_reasoning.egg_pattern: Recent stocking makes eggs a logical confidence fly.
fly_reasoning.squirmy_worm: A simple stocked-trout attractor and good dirty-water option.
fly_reasoning.duracell: A strong attractor nymph when fish will move a little but are not fully aggressive.
fly_reasoning.zebra_midge: Small and natural for clear, pressured, technical conditions.
fly_reasoning.pheasant_tail: A reliable natural nymph when trout are feeding but cautious.

caution_notes: Bright/high-pressure conditions may make fish cover-oriented and less forgiving.
reasoning: High pressure and low cloud cover make this fishable but technical.

generated_text.quick_read: Trout fishing on the Soque River is fair with technical conditions due to high pressure and low cloud cover. Recent stocking supports using eggs, worms, and attractor nymphs for best results.
generated_text.best_window: Day 1 morning is the best window because stable high pressure and low wind provide ideal, though technical, fishing conditions with trout favoring shade and deeper runs.
generated_text.current_conditions: USGS reports a flow of 65.1 cfs and gage height of 3.56 ft; water temperature and turbidity data are unavailable.
generated_text.weather_setup: Sky cover averages around 20%, pressure is high at 30.11 inHg with stable trend unknown, wind is light from the east at 2.2 mph, and rain probability is low at 14%.
generated_text.what_this_means: Trout will hold near shade, undercut banks, and deeper runs to stay comfortable in bright, high-pressure conditions. Use smaller natural flies and careful drifts with less aggressive presentations, and prioritize stocked-trout attractors like egg pattern, squirmy worm, and duracell. Exercise caution as fish may be cover-oriented and selective.
